$comment: Canonical glossary of grammatical terms used in Linguics explanations. The housing's renderer wraps matched terms in interactive spans (hover for the short definition). Authors propose additions via glossary_suggestions_<topic>.json; the project author merges accepted suggestions into this file. Chats do not edit this file directly.

clitic.short: A small pronoun that attaches to the verb position. Italian's mi/ti/lo/la/ci/vi/li/le/gli/ne are clitics.
clitic.long: A clitic is a pronoun that doesn't stand alone; it leans on a verb. In Italian, finite verbs take clitics before them ('lo vedo'); infinitives, gerunds, and tu/noi/voi imperatives take them attached at the end ('vederlo', 'dimmelo'). When two or more clitics combine they form a cluster with vowel-change and ordering rules.

clitic cluster.short: Two or more clitics stacked together, with vowel changes (mi to me before another clitic) and a fixed order (IOP before DOP).
clitic cluster.long: Italian commonly combines an indirect object pronoun with a direct object pronoun ('me lo dai'), or with the partitive ne ('me ne hai dato due'). When stacked, mi/ti/ci/vi change to me/te/ce/ve; gli and le both become glie- and join the following clitic into a single written word (glielo, gliela, glieli, gliele, gliene).

direct object pronoun.short: A pronoun that replaces a direct object: mi/ti/lo/la/ci/vi/li/le.
direct object pronoun.long: The direct object is the thing or person the verb acts on directly. 'I see Maria' becomes 'la vedo' (la = her, the direct object). Forms: mi, ti, lo (m.sg), la (f.sg), ci (1pl), vi (2pl), li (m.pl), le (f.pl). Singular lo/la elide to l' before a vowel. Position is before a finite verb, attached to an infinitive/gerund/tu-imperative.

indirect object pronoun.short: A pronoun for the 'to/for someone' role: mi/ti/gli/le/ci/vi/gli (or loro).
indirect object pronoun.long: The indirect object is who the action is for, or to. 'I gave the book to Maria' becomes 'le ho dato il libro' (le = to her). Forms: mi, ti, gli (m.sg or 3pl in modern usage), le (f.sg), ci (1pl), vi (2pl). Formal Lei takes the capitalised Le. Some verbs (piacere, mancare, sembrare) use IOPs where English would use a subject.

reflexive pronoun.short: A pronoun pointing back at the subject: mi/ti/si/ci/vi/si.
reflexive pronoun.long: Used with reflexive verbs (lavarsi, alzarsi) where the subject acts on itself. The third-person form si is the same for singular and plural and across genders. Reflexive verbs take essere as their auxiliary in compound tenses, and the participle agrees with the subject.

subject pronoun.short: io, tu, lui/lei/Lei, noi, voi, loro. Often dropped in Italian because the verb ending shows the subject.
subject pronoun.long: Italian is a pro-drop language; the subject pronoun is optional when the verb conjugation already shows the person and number. The subject pronoun is used for emphasis, contrast, or disambiguation. The full set: io, tu, lui/lei (informal Lei, formal), noi, voi, loro.

stressed pronoun.short: A pronoun that can take stress and stand alone, used for emphasis: me, te, lui, lei, noi, voi, loro.
stressed pronoun.long: Also called disjunctive pronouns. Used after prepositions ('a me', 'con te'), for contrast ('vedo lui, non lei'), or when the verb has already been mentioned and only the pronoun is needed. Contrasts with clitic pronouns, which can't be stressed and must attach to a verb.

neuter lo.short: lo standing in for a whole clause or for a predicate, invariable regardless of gender: 'Lo so' = I know (it).
neuter lo.long: When 'lo' resumes an entire clause ('Sai che piove?' 'Sì, lo so.') or a predicate adjective ('Sei stanca?' 'Lo sono.'), it stays as 'lo' regardless of the gender of the antecedent. Distinct from masculine-singular DOP lo, although the surface form is identical.

vowel change.short: When mi/ti/ci/vi change to me/te/ce/ve before another clitic.
vowel change.long: In a clitic cluster, the final -i of the indirect-object pronouns mi, ti, ci, vi shifts to -e before any following clitic (DOP or ne): mi + lo becomes me lo; ti + ne becomes te ne. The same shift applies to the reflexive si in the cluster pattern (si + lo becomes se lo) and to existential/locative ci (ci + ne becomes ce ne). This is purely a writing/spelling rule; the pronunciation follows.

consonant doubling.short: When a clitic attaches to a monosyllabic imperative (da', di', fa', va', sta'), the clitic's initial consonant doubles: dammi, dimmi, fammelo, vacci, stammi.
consonant doubling.long: The five tu imperatives da', di', fa', va', sta' double the initial consonant of the attaching clitic: da' + mi gives dammi (not dami), fa' + me + lo gives fammelo, va' + ci gives vacci. Exception: gli does not double (di' + gli gives digli, not diggli). The rule is graphic but reflects spoken Italian.

monosyllabic imperative.short: The five tu imperatives da', di', fa', va', sta'. They double the next consonant when a clitic attaches.
monosyllabic imperative.long: Italian's five monosyllabic tu imperatives (from dare, dire, fare, andare, stare) drop the verb's full form and end in a vowel plus apostrophe. When a clitic attaches, the initial consonant of the clitic doubles: dammi, dimmi, fammelo, vacci, stammi. The exception is gli, which doesn't double: digli, not diggli.

glie-cluster.short: The combined form when IOP gli/le meets a DOP or ne: gli/le + lo/la/li/le/ne giving glielo/gliela/glieli/gliele/gliene.
glie-cluster.long: When the IOP gli (m.sg or m.pl in modern usage) or le (f.sg or formal Lei) combines with a DOP or with ne, both clitics fuse into a single word starting with glie-. The IOP gender disappears: glielo can be 'it to him' or 'it to her' or 'it to you formal'. Unlike other pre-verb clusters (me lo, te ne), glie-clusters are always one written word in any position.

joined cluster.short: When two written words become one: vederlo (attached to infinitive), dammelo (attached to imperative).
joined cluster.long: Clitics attaching to infinitives, gerunds, and tu/noi/voi imperatives join with the verb (and with each other) into a single written word. The cluster's vowel-change and ordering rules still apply: vedermelo, parlandogliene, fammelo. Glielo-family clusters (glielo, gliela, gliene) are always one word in any position. Other pre-verb clusters (me lo, te ne, ce la) stay as two words.

separated cluster.short: A pre-verb clitic cluster written as two words: me lo dai, te ne ho parlato, ce la fai.
separated cluster.long: Pre-verb clitic clusters in finite tenses stay as two separate words in Italian's standard spelling, even though they're pronounced together. Joining them into one word (melo, tene, cela) is a common over-generalisation from the post-verb attached pattern. Glielo-family clusters are the exception: they're always one word.

pre-verb position.short: The default slot for a clitic in finite tenses: immediately before the (auxiliary or finite) verb. 'Lo vedo'.
pre-verb position.long: In present, imperfect, passato prossimo, future, conditional, subjunctive, the clitic sits immediately before the verb (or before the auxiliary, in compound tenses). Negation, when present, goes outside: 'non lo vedo'. The pre-verb position is the unmarked one; everything else (attached, between) follows different rules.

attached position.short: When the clitic attaches to the end of an infinitive, gerund, or tu/noi/voi imperative: 'vederlo', 'dammelo'.
attached position.long: Infinitives drop their final -e and the clitic attaches as one written word. Gerunds attach in the same way. Tu/noi/voi positive imperatives attach the clitic. Monosyllabic tu imperatives (da', di', fa', va', sta') double the initial consonant of the attaching clitic. Formal Lei imperatives do NOT attach; they keep the clitic pre-verb.

negative imperative.short: Italian's 'don't do X' form: non + infinitive for tu, regular imperative for noi/voi/formal.
negative imperative.long: The negative tu imperative uses non + infinitive ('non parlare!'), NOT the positive imperative form. Noi and voi use 'non' plus the regular imperative ('non parliamo!', 'non parlate!'). Formal Lei uses 'non' plus the subjunctive ('non parli!'). With clitics: the tu form has two valid positions (attached to the infinitive, or pre-verb).

antecedent.short: The earlier noun phrase that a pronoun refers back to.
antecedent.long: When ne stands for 'three of them' or lo stands for 'it', the antecedent is the noun phrase the pronoun is pointing at: 'Hai delle mele? Sì, ne ho mangiate tre.' Here the antecedent is 'mele' (f.pl), which is why the participle is mangiate. The antecedent's gender and number control participle agreement on the verb.

resumption.short: Following up a fronted noun phrase with a clitic that points back to it: 'La pizza, l'ho mangiata.'
resumption.long: When a noun phrase is moved to the front of the sentence (or sometimes the end), Italian requires a clitic later in the sentence to fill the role it would normally have played. In 'La pizza, l'ho mangiata', la pizza is fronted and the clitic l' resumes it as the direct object of mangiare. Spoken Italian uses resumption far more readily than English; in writing it's marked but standard.

right-dislocation.short: Putting a topic at the END of the sentence with a clitic referring back to it earlier.
right-dislocation.long: Mirror of left-dislocation: 'L'ho mangiata, la pizza.' The clitic comes first, then the noun phrase is added at the end as a clarifier. Very common in spoken Italian; less marked in writing than left-dislocation.

experiencer.short: The person who feels or undergoes the action of verbs like piacere, mancare, sembrare. Surfaces as an IOP in Italian.
experiencer.long: In English we say 'I like pizza' (the experiencer is the subject). In Italian we say 'Mi piace la pizza' (the experiencer 'I' surfaces as the IOP 'mi'; the pizza is the grammatical subject and the verb agrees with it). The piacere-family verbs (piacere, mancare, sembrare, bastare, servire, occorrere) all follow this pattern.

argument structure.short: The set of slots a verb opens up: subject, direct object, indirect object, prepositional complement.
argument structure.long: A verb's argument structure determines which pronouns it can take. Transitive verbs (vedere, mangiare) take a direct object and so a DOP. Verbs with 'a + person' (telefonare, parlare, dare) take an indirect object and so an IOP. Verbs with 'di + topic' (parlare di, pensare di) take a di-complement and so pronominal ne. The argument structure is a lexical fact about the verb; it isn't always obvious from the English equivalent.

pro-drop.short: Italian regularly omits the subject pronoun because the verb ending shows who's speaking.
pro-drop.long: Italian, like Spanish and Latin, is a pro-drop language: 'parlo' alone means 'I speak' (the -o ending shows 1sg). Subject pronouns (io, tu, lui, lei) are used for emphasis, contrast, or disambiguation. The opposite of English, which obligatorily marks the subject.

lexicalised.short: When a clitic (or clitic cluster) is built into the verb's identity rather than referring to anything: farcela, andarsene.
lexicalised.long: Some Italian verbs incorporate clitics as part of their lexical form. The la in farcela doesn't refer to any antecedent; the ne in andarsene doesn't mean 'of them'. The clitics are fossilised parts of the verb. The reflexive component varies by person (me ne vado, te ne vai, se ne va), but the ne is fixed.

pronominal verb.short: A verb that always carries built-in clitics: andarsene, farcela, fregarsene.
pronominal verb.long: Pronominal verbs are lexicalised forms with one or more clitics baked into the dictionary entry. Andarsene = andare + si + ne (to leave); farcela = fare + ci + la (to manage). The clitics vary the reflexive part with person but otherwise stay in place. Sometimes called 'verbi pronominali' or 'verbi procomplementari'.

reciprocal.short: Action done to each other, marked by the reflexive plural: 'ci vediamo' = we see each other.
reciprocal.long: Italian uses the reflexive form (plural persons) to express reciprocal action. 'Si amano' can mean 'they love themselves' (reflexive) or 'they love each other' (reciprocal); context decides. The reciprocal reading is more common with plurals. The construction takes essere as auxiliary in compound tenses, like all reflexives.

preceding-DOP rule.short: The rule that the past participle agrees with a preceding direct-object clitic in a compound tense: 'l'ho mangiata'.
preceding-DOP rule.long: With avere as auxiliary, the past participle is invariable by default. But when a direct-object clitic (lo/la/li/le or partitive ne) comes before the auxiliary, the participle agrees with it in gender and number: 'Ho visto Anna' becomes 'L'ho vista' (f.sg agreement). The rule with 1/2-person DOPs (mi, ti, ci, vi) is optional: agreement feels register-marked.

ne (partitive).short: The clitic that means 'of them / some of it' after a quantity: ne ho tre.
ne (partitive).long: Partitive ne replaces a noun phrase with a quantity attached: 'Quanti libri hai?' 'Ne ho tre.' / 'Hai del vino?' 'Ne ho un po'.' Triggers participle agreement in passato prossimo ('ne ho mangiate tre' if antecedent is f.pl). Distinct from pronominal ne (= di + topic), which does NOT trigger agreement.

ne (pronominal).short: The clitic that stands for a di-phrase: parlare di becomes ne.
ne (pronominal).long: Replaces a 'di + topic' complement: 'Parli del libro?' 'Sì, ne parlo.' / 'Pensi di Maria?' 'Sì, ne penso bene.' Does NOT trigger participle agreement (only partitive ne does). Used with verbs and adjectives that take a di-complement: parlare di, pensare di, essere sicuro di, avere bisogno di.

fronting.short: Putting the topic of a sentence at the start, before the verb.
fronting.long: Italian commonly puts what you're talking about at the very start of the sentence. If the fronted thing is a direct object, the object must be resumed later in the sentence with a clitic pronoun ('quel libro, lo voglio leggere'). The technical name for the construction is left-dislocation.

left-dislocation.short: Same as fronting: putting a topic at the start, with a pronoun referring back to it later.
left-dislocation.long: The construction where an element (typically a direct object) sits at the start of the sentence and is later 'resumed' by a clitic pronoun. Often punctuated with a comma in writing; in speech, prosody marks the break. Italian uses this construction far more readily than English.

elision.short: Dropping a final vowel before a following vowel: lo + ho becomes l'ho.
elision.long: Italian routinely elides the final vowel of certain words when followed by a vowel. Singular clitics lo and la elide to l' before a verb starting with a vowel ('l'ho visto'). The indefinite article una elides to un' before a feminine vowel-initial noun ('un'amica'). Plural li and le do NOT elide.

agreement.short: Two words sharing their gender and number: la casa bianca (both feminine singular).
agreement.long: Italian adjectives agree with their nouns in gender and number, articles agree with their nouns, past participles often agree with the subject or with a preceding direct-object pronoun. Agreement is a major source of misses for English-speaking learners.

participle agreement.short: When the past participle has to agree with the subject (essere) or a preceding DOP/ne (avere).
participle agreement.long: With essere as auxiliary, the participle agrees with the subject ('Maria è andata'). With avere, the participle is invariable by default ('ho mangiato la pasta'), but agrees with a preceding direct object pronoun or with preceding ne ('l'ho mangiata', 'ne ho mangiate tre').

preceding partitive.short: When the partitive ne precedes the verb in a compound tense, the past participle agrees with the implicit referent.
preceding partitive.long: Italian's partitive pronoun ne triggers participle agreement when it precedes the verb: 'Hai mangiato delle mele? Sì, ne ho mangiate tre' (mangiate agrees with mele, f.pl). Without preceding ne, the participle is invariable: 'Ho mangiato tre mele'. The rule parallels preceding-DOP agreement.

past participle.short: The verb form used to build compound tenses: parlato, venduto, dormito, fatto.
past participle.long: Formed regularly: -are gives -ato (parlato), -ere gives -uto (venduto), -ire gives -ito (dormito). Many -ere verbs have irregular participles (fare to fatto, dire to detto, prendere to preso). Used with avere or essere to form passato prossimo, trapassato, and other compound tenses.

auxiliary.short: The 'helper' verb in a compound tense: ho/hai/ha or sono/sei/è.
auxiliary.long: Italian compound tenses use avere (have) or essere (be) plus a past participle. Transitive verbs and most intransitives take avere; intransitive verbs of motion or change, reflexives, and some others (piacere, bastare, sembrare) take essere. With essere, the participle agrees with the subject.

default avere.short: The unmarked auxiliary choice in the passato prossimo. Used for transitive verbs and most intransitives that don't fit the motion/change/reflexive classes.
default avere.long: Most Italian verbs take avere as their auxiliary in compound tenses: 'ho mangiato', 'hanno lavorato', 'abbiamo parlato'. Essere is used only for the marked classes (verbs of motion, change of state, reflexive verbs, and a few others like piacere, bastare, mancare). When in doubt, avere is the default.

modal verb.short: A verb that governs another verb in the infinitive: dovere (must), potere (can), volere (want), often sapere (know how to).
modal verb.long: Italian modals (dovere, potere, volere, sometimes sapere) take an infinitive complement: 'Devo studiare', 'Posso uscire?', 'Voglio mangiare'. Two behaviours come up in Linguics. First, in compound tenses, the modal can keep its own default avere or inherit the auxiliary of the infinitive (see modal auxiliary inheritance). Second, clitics governed by the infinitive can sit before the modal ('lo voglio vedere') or attach to the infinitive ('voglio vederlo'); both correct, never between.

modal auxiliary inheritance.short: A modal verb (dovere, potere, volere) taking the auxiliary of the infinitive it governs instead of its own default avere.
modal auxiliary inheritance.long: When a modal precedes an infinitive that selects essere (andare, partire, arrivare, reflexives), prescriptive Italian has the modal also take essere: 'sono dovuto andare', 'è voluta partire' (with participle agreement). Colloquial Italian increasingly uses avere with the modal regardless: 'ho dovuto andare'. Both are now widely accepted; the prescriptive form is preferred in writing.

person agreement.short: Getting the auxiliary form right for the subject's person and number, distinct from getting the auxiliary family right.
person agreement.long: In a compound tense, the auxiliary must match the subject's person and number: 'Marco ha parlato' (3sg), not 'Marco hanno parlato' (3pl). A learner can pick the right auxiliary family (avere) but produce the wrong form. The Linguics taxonomy distinguishes auxiliary family choice from auxiliary person agreement because the errors have different remedies.

adverb placement.short: In compound tenses, certain adverbs (già, ancora, mai, sempre, appena) sit between the auxiliary and the past participle.
adverb placement.long: Italian places a small set of high-frequency time/frequency adverbs between the auxiliary and the past participle: 'ho già mangiato', 'non sono mai stato a Roma', 'sono appena arrivata'. Anglophone learners often produce 'ho mangiato già' or 'già ho mangiato', both wrong. The rule applies to già, ancora, mai, sempre, appena, anche, and a few others.

negation placement.short: In compound tenses, 'non' sits immediately before the auxiliary: 'non ho mangiato', not 'ho non mangiato'.
negation placement.long: Italian places 'non' before the entire verb cluster, including the auxiliary. Anglophone learners often produce 'ho non mangiato' on the model of 'have not eaten', which is wrong. Adverbs like ancora, mai, sempre may follow 'non' and the auxiliary: 'non ho ancora finito', 'non sono mai stato a Roma'.

tense mapping.short: The rule for choosing English tense when translating an Italian passato prossimo: simple past when a definite past-time adverb is present, present perfect otherwise.
tense mapping.long: Italian passato prossimo covers ground that English splits into simple past and present perfect. With a definite past-time adverb (ieri, l'anno scorso, due giorni fa), English uses simple past: 'I ate yesterday'. With durative or up-to-now adverbs (mai, ancora, già, appena), English uses present perfect: 'I've never eaten sushi'. Present perfect with a definite past-time adverb is ungrammatical in English.

tu-X collapse.short: When a subject combines 'tu' with another third-person element (tu e Maria), the verb takes the voi (2pl) form.
tu-X collapse.long: Italian collapses a mixed second-and-third-person subject to second-plural: 'Tu e Maria avete visto il film' (not hanno). The same pattern applies to 'io e X' collapsing to noi. The diagnostic miss is defaulting to a third-person form because of the third-person component (Maria).

stem expansion.short: When a verb's contracted infinitive 'opens out' into a longer stem in the past participle.
stem expansion.long: Several Italian verbs are descended from longer Latin infinitives that contracted over time: bere from bevere, dire from dicere, fare from facere, porre from ponere, tradurre from traducere. Their past participles use the older, expanded stem: bevuto, detto, fatto, posto, tradotto. The contracted-infinitive class is small but high-frequency.

ambiguous auxiliary.short: A verb that takes avere when used transitively and essere when used intransitively.
ambiguous auxiliary.long: Some Italian verbs change auxiliary with use: correre, passare, cambiare, saltare, volare. 'Ho corso la maratona' (transitive, ran the race, so avere); 'sono corso a casa' (intransitive, motion, so essere). The choice reflects whether the verb is acting on a direct object or describing motion or change.

verb of motion.short: An intransitive verb describing directed movement: andare, venire, partire, arrivare, uscire, entrare, salire, scendere, tornare.
verb of motion.long: Italian verbs of motion take essere as their auxiliary in compound tenses, and the past participle agrees with the subject. The class is fairly closed. Often abbreviated as 'motion intransitive' in technical descriptions.

change of state.short: A verb describing a transition: nascere (be born), morire (die), diventare (become), restare (remain).
change of state.long: Italian verbs of change of state take essere as their auxiliary in compound tenses, like verbs of motion. The past participle agrees with the subject. Examples: nascere (sono nato), morire (è morto), diventare (sono diventato), restare (sono restato).

first conjugation.short: Italian's -are verb class. The largest and most regular conjugation: parlare, mangiare, lavorare.
first conjugation.long: The vast majority of Italian verbs end in -are. They follow predictable patterns across all tenses: present parlo/parli/parla/parliamo/parlate/parlano; passato prossimo participle in -ato. New verbs entering Italian (chattare, googlare) join this class by default.

second conjugation.short: Italian's -ere verb class. Many have irregular past participles: prendere giving preso, scrivere giving scritto, vedere giving visto.
second conjugation.long: Italian -ere verbs are mostly the descendants of Latin's second and third conjugations. The regular ones form -uto: venduto, ricevuto, creduto. Common irregulars: prendere/preso, mettere/messo, scrivere/scritto, leggere/letto, vedere/visto, fare/fatto. About a third of -ere verbs are irregular.

third conjugation.short: Italian's -ire verb class. Regular past participle in -ito (dormito, finito, sentito).
third conjugation.long: The smallest of the three Italian conjugations. Regular past participle in -ito. Many -ire verbs use the -isc- pattern in the present tense (capire giving capisco). A few have irregular participles: aprire/aperto, venire/venuto, morire/morto.

-isc- pattern.short: An infix used by some -ire verbs in the present tense: capire conjugates capisco/capisci/capisce/capiamo/capite/capiscono.
-isc- pattern.long: A subset of Italian -ire verbs insert -isc- between the stem and the ending in the three singular forms and the third-person plural of the present tense: capire, finire, preferire, pulire, spedire. The infix does not appear in the noi/voi forms (capiamo, capite) or in the past participle (regular capito). Taught alongside -ire conjugation as a near-default for many common -ire verbs.

compound tense.short: A tense built from an auxiliary (avere or essere) plus a past participle: passato prossimo, trapassato, etc.
compound tense.long: Italian compound tenses pair an auxiliary in some form (present for passato prossimo, imperfect for trapassato) with the past participle. Clitics in compound tenses sit before the auxiliary ('l'ho visto'), not between auxiliary and participle.

infinitive.short: The dictionary form of a verb: parlare, vedere, dormire.
infinitive.long: Italian infinitives end in -are, -ere, or -ire. Clitics attach to the end of an infinitive, with the final -e of the infinitive dropping: vedere + lo becomes vederlo, parlare + gli becomes parlargli.

gerund.long: Italian's gerund corresponds roughly to English's -ing form in some uses. Used with stare to form progressive aspect ('sto parlando'), or alone to express manner or contemporaneous action ('parlando con lui, ho capito'). Clitics attach to the end of the gerund: vedendolo, parlandogli.

imperative.short: The command form: parla! (informal singular), parli! (formal), parliamo!, parlate!.
imperative.long: Used for direct commands. The tu/noi/voi positive imperative attaches clitics ('dimmi!', 'parliamone!'). The formal Lei imperative is built from the subjunctive and keeps clitics before the verb ('mi dica!'). Negative tu imperatives use 'non' + infinitive, with clitics either before or attached.

subjunctive.short: The 'irrealis' mood, used after certain verbs and conjunctions: che io parli, che lui veda.
subjunctive.long: Marks doubt, opinion, possibility, or subordination after certain triggers (penso che, prima che, sebbene, etc.). Italian has present, imperfect, past, and pluperfect subjunctive. Formal Lei imperatives use the present subjunctive form.

present indicative.short: The basic present tense: io parlo, tu parli, lui parla.
present indicative.long: Used for ongoing actions, habits, general truths, and (in Italian, more readily than in English) future actions when the timeframe is clear. The 'default' tense; a learner's home base.

imperfect indicative.short: The 'background' past tense: io parlavo, lui andava. Used for habitual or ongoing past actions.
imperfect indicative.long: Marks habitual, repeated, or ongoing past actions, descriptions, and background states. Contrasts with passato prossimo (single completed actions). 'Quando ero piccolo, parlavo solo italiano' uses imperfect for the ongoing childhood state.

passato prossimo.short: The compound past tense: ho parlato, sono andata.
passato prossimo.long: Formed with avere or essere (in the present indicative) plus the past participle. Italian's most common past tense in speech and most writing. Maps roughly to English present perfect ('I have spoken') or simple past ('I spoke') depending on whether a definite past-time adverb is present.

trapassato prossimo.short: The 'had done' past tense: avevo parlato, ero andato.
trapassato prossimo.long: Formed with the imperfect of avere or essere plus a past participle. Used for actions completed before another past action: 'Quando sono arrivato, Marco se n'era già andato' (by the time I arrived, Marco had already left).

future.short: The future tense: parlerò, vedrò. Clitics behave the same as in present.
future.long: Used for upcoming actions and for probability ('Saranno le tre' = 'it must be three o'clock'). Italian also uses the present indicative very commonly for near-future events. Clitics sit pre-verb just as in any finite tense.

conditional.short: The 'would' form: parlerei, parleresti. Used for politeness, hypotheticals, and reported future-in-the-past.
conditional.long: Present conditional (parlerei) and past conditional (avrei parlato). Used for politeness ('vorrei un caffè'), hypotheticals ('se potessi, andrei'), and to express what would happen. Clitics behave the same as in any finite tense (pre-verb).

reflexive verb.short: A verb where the subject acts on itself: lavarsi (to wash oneself), alzarsi (to get up).
reflexive verb.long: Italian reflexive verbs are conjugated with a reflexive pronoun: mi lavo, ti alzi, si sveglia. The infinitive carries -si at the end (lavarsi). All reflexive verbs take essere as their auxiliary in compound tenses, and the participle agrees with the subject.

transitive verb.short: A verb that takes a direct object: 'mangiare la pasta' (eat pasta).
transitive verb.long: Italian transitive verbs take avere as their auxiliary. Some verbs are transitive in some uses and intransitive in others (correre transitive = to run a race, intransitive = to move quickly), and auxiliary choice can shift accordingly.

intransitive verb.short: A verb that doesn't take a direct object: 'andare' (to go), 'arrivare' (to arrive).
intransitive verb.long: Most intransitive verbs of motion or change-of-state take essere as their auxiliary (sono andato, è arrivata, sono nato). The participle then agrees with the subject. Other intransitives (dormire, parlare) take avere.

predicate.short: The part of the sentence that says something about the subject: in 'Maria is tired', the predicate is 'is tired'.
predicate.long: When the predicate is replaced by 'lo' (neuter), the form is invariable: 'Sono stanca' becomes 'Lo sono'. Predicate adjectives, predicate nouns, and whole verb phrases can all be resumed this way.

articulated preposition.short: A preposition fused with a definite article: a + il = al, di + la = della, in + i = nei.
articulated preposition.long: Italian fuses the prepositions a, di, da, in, su (and sometimes con, per) with the following definite article into a single word. The full set: al/allo/alla/all'/ai/agli/alle; del/dello/della/dell'/dei/degli/delle; and analogously for da, in, su. Memorising the table is an A2-B1 task.

definite article.short: Italian's 'the': il, lo, la, l', i, gli, le.
definite article.long: Used more often than English 'the' (with abstract nouns, body parts, general categories). Form depends on gender, number, and what sound the following word starts with: il libro, lo studente, l'amico, la casa, i libri, gli studenti, le case.

indefinite article.long: Used in the singular: un libro (m before consonant), uno studente (m before s+consonant or z), una casa (f before consonant), un'amica (f before vowel). No indefinite plural; Italian uses dei/delle (a partitive) or a bare noun instead.

partitive.short: An expression meaning 'some of', often the pronoun ne or the preposition di + article (del, della, dei, delle).
partitive.long: The partitive pronoun ne replaces a noun phrase introduced by 'some' or by a quantity ('Quanti libri hai?' 'Ne ho tre.'). The partitive article (del, dello, della, dei, degli, delle) means 'some' before nouns ('del pane', 'delle mele').

locative.short: Referring to a place. The clitic ci can mean 'there' (and ne can mean 'from there').
locative.long: The clitic ci has many functions; one is locative ('Vai a Roma?' 'Sì, ci vado.'). Distinct from DOP/IOP/reflexive ci. Also appears in existentials (c'è / ci sono = there is / there are) and in idiomatic verbs (tenerci, metterci, volerci, farcela).

formal address.short: Using Lei (third-person singular forms) instead of tu when addressing someone respectfully.
formal address.long: Italian distinguishes informal tu from formal Lei. Lei takes third-person-singular verbs and uses the pronouns La (DOP) and Le (IOP), often capitalised in writing. The formal imperative uses the subjunctive form. The plural formal (Loro) is rare in modern Italian; voi is used instead.

register.short: The level of formality of language: formal, neutral, informal, colloquial.
register.long: Italian has clear register distinctions: pronouns (Lei vs tu), greetings (buongiorno vs ciao), vocabulary choices (parlare vs chiacchierare). Translation items can specify the target register; the learner is graded against that register.

Class I adjective.short: An -o-ending adjective: rosso, alta, italiani, nuove. Four forms: -o/-a/-i/-e.
Class I adjective.long: The most common Italian adjective class. Four forms: masculine singular -o, feminine singular -a, masculine plural -i, feminine plural -e. Most descriptive adjectives are Class I.

Class II adjective.short: An -e-ending adjective: grande, intelligente, felice. Two forms: -e (sg) / -i (pl).
Class II adjective.long: Class II adjectives have the same form for masculine and feminine singular (-e) and for masculine and feminine plural (-i). About a third of Italian adjectives are Class II.

stem change.short: A spelling change in the stem of a Class I adjective when forming the plural, to keep a sound or drop an unstressed -i.
stem change.long: Italian Class I adjectives ending in -co, -ca, -go, -ga sometimes insert an h in the plural to preserve the hard c/g sound (bianco/bianchi, lunga/lunghe). Adjectives ending in -cio, -gio, -cia, -gia drop the unstressed -i when forming the plural (vecchio/vecchi, grigia/grige). Whether the h appears can depend on stress (bianco/bianchi but simpatico/simpatici). A small set of words are irregular (amico/amici despite stress, antico/antichi despite stress).

h-insertion.short: Inserting an h in the plural of a Class I adjective ending in -co, -ca, -go, -ga to keep the hard c or g sound (bianco/bianchi).
h-insertion.long: Italian doesn't have a separate letter for the hard-c-before-i sound, so an h is written in: bianco/bianchi (not bianci, which would soften the c). Same for -ga/-ghe (lunga/lunghe). The rule is reliable when stress falls on the penult (bianco, lungo), but the antepenult-stress group goes the other way (simpatico/simpatici with soft c). The most common kind of stem change.

penultimate stress.short: Stress on the second-to-last syllable. Bianco (bian-co) has penultimate stress.
penultimate stress.long: Most Italian words have penultimate stress (parola, finestra, bambino). The 'penult' is the second-to-last syllable. Stress position matters for the -co/-go pluralisation rule: penult-stressed adjectives take -chi/-ghi (bianco/bianchi), antepenult-stressed take -ci/-gi (simpatico/simpatici).

antepenultimate stress.short: Stress on the third-to-last syllable. Simpatico (sim-pa-ti-co) has antepenultimate stress.
antepenultimate stress.long: Less common than penultimate stress, but a stable pattern in adjectives like simpatico, fantastico, pratico, magico. The 'antepenult' is the third-to-last syllable. Antepenult-stressed adjectives in -co take the soft-c plural -ci, in contrast to penult-stressed adjectives which take -chi.

predicative position.short: When the adjective comes after a copular verb (essere, sembrare): 'la casa è rossa'.
predicative position.long: The adjective sits on the other side of a linking verb from its subject. 'Il libro è nuovo', 'sembra felice'. The adjective still agrees in gender and number with the subject. Contrasts with attributive position, where the adjective sits inside the noun phrase next to the noun.

attributive position.short: When the adjective sits next to its noun in a noun phrase: 'la casa rossa'.
attributive position.long: The adjective is part of the noun phrase, either before or after the noun. 'Una macchina nuova' (post-noun), 'un bravo studente' (pre-noun). Contrasts with predicative position, where the adjective sits across a linking verb from the subject.

pre-noun position.short: The adjective sits before the noun: 'un bel libro', 'una piccola casa'.
pre-noun position.long: A small set of Italian adjectives (bello, buono, grande, piccolo, nuovo, vecchio, giovane, bravo and a few others) canonically sit before the noun in their unmarked use. For most descriptive adjectives (colours, nationalities, categories), pre-noun position is marked or stylistically loaded.

post-noun position.short: The adjective sits after the noun: 'la casa rossa', 'un libro interessante'. The default for most descriptive adjectives.
post-noun position.long: Most Italian adjectives sit after the noun. Colours, nationalities, shapes, categories, and most evaluative adjectives default here. A small set of common adjectives (bello, buono, grande, etc.) prefer pre-noun position in their unmarked use.

unmarked position.short: The position an adjective takes when there's no special emphasis or shift in meaning. 'Un bel libro' is unmarked; 'un libro bello' is marked.
unmarked position.long: For most descriptive adjectives, the unmarked position is post-noun. For the small set including bello, buono, grande, piccolo, nuovo, vecchio, the unmarked position is pre-noun. Marked positions are still grammatical but carry an emphasis or meaning shift the unmarked version doesn't.

semantic shift.short: A change in meaning depending on whether the adjective sits before or after the noun. 'Un vecchio amico' (long-standing friend) vs 'un amico vecchio' (elderly friend).
semantic shift.long: A subset of pre/post-noun adjectives carry different readings in the two positions. Pre-noun is usually figurative or relational (un caro amico = beloved); post-noun is usually literal (un amico caro = expensive). Classic cases: vecchio, grande, povero, caro, nuovo, unico. A real source of meaning-misses for English-speaking learners.

allomorph.short: A variant form of the same word, conditioned by the surrounding context. Bello has the allomorphs bel, bello, bell', bei, begli depending on what follows.
allomorph.long: The pre-noun form of bello, quello, buono, and santo varies depending on what the following noun starts with, paralleling the form of the article. Bello becomes bel libro (il), bello zaino (lo), bell'amico (l'), bei libri (i), begli zaini (gli). The technical name for these context-conditioned variants is 'allomorphs'.

mixed-gender plural.short: When a plural noun phrase contains both genders, the adjective takes masculine plural form: 'i ragazzi e le ragazze sono italiani'.
mixed-gender plural.long: Italian (like most Romance languages) defaults to masculine plural agreement when the antecedent is mixed-gender. This holds even when the feminines outnumber the masculines. A common English-influenced miss is proximity-agreement (agreeing with the closest noun), which gives the wrong italiane.

invariable adjective.short: An adjective that doesn't change form for gender or number: rosa, blu, viola, marrone, beige, pari.
invariable adjective.long: A small but important set of Italian adjectives never inflect. Colour terms borrowed from nouns (rosa = the flower, blu, viola, marrone, beige, fucsia, lilla, indaco) are invariable: 'le case rosa', 'i pantaloni blu'. Also pari, dispari, impari. Two-word compound colours (verde scuro, blu marino) are invariable as a unit.

compound colour.short: A two-word colour expression that stays invariable as a unit: verde scuro, blu marino, giallo limone.
compound colour.long: When a colour is qualified by a second word (light, dark, navy, lemon), the whole expression becomes invariable. 'Le pareti verde scuro' not 'verdi scure'. The same compound rule applies to most two-word colour expressions in Italian.

1sg.long: Shorthand for 'first-person singular' in grammatical descriptions. Verb form for io, e.g. parlo, sono andato.

2sg.long: Shorthand for 'second-person singular' (informal). Verb form for tu, e.g. parli, sei andato. Distinct from formal Lei, which uses third-person-singular forms.

3sg.short: Third-person singular (he, she, it; Italian lui, lei, Lei, esso).
3sg.long: Third-person singular: in Italian, the verb form for lui/lei/Lei (and esso/essa). E.g., parla (he/she speaks), è andato (he has gone).

1pl.long: Shorthand for 'first-person plural'. Verb form for noi, e.g. parliamo, siamo andati.

2pl.long: Shorthand for 'second-person plural'. Verb form for voi, e.g. parlate, siete andati. Also used in modern Italian for formal-plural address (Loro is rare).

3pl.long: Shorthand for 'third-person plural'. Verb form for loro, e.g. parlano, sono andati.

m.sg.long: Shorthand for 'masculine singular' in grammatical descriptions.

f.sg.long: Shorthand for 'feminine singular' in grammatical descriptions.

m.pl.long: Shorthand for 'masculine plural' in grammatical descriptions.

f.pl.long: Shorthand for 'feminine plural' in grammatical descriptions.
